text: BERGA, a small town of Sachsen-Weimar, on the r. bank of the Elster, 12 m. NE of Neustadt, and 16 m. E of Jena. Pop. 556.—Also a town of Prussian Saxony, in the circle of Merseburg, W of Rossla. Pop. 1,140.—Also a small town of Spain, in Catalonia, near the river Llobregat, 12 m. E of Solsona, and 25 m. N of Manresa.-Also a small town of Norway, 60 m. ENE of Christiania.-Also a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Ticino, 15 m. WNW of Locarno.

BERGAMO, a delegation or province of the Lombardo-Venetian kingdom, in the gov. of Milan, which takes its name from its capital. It has the Valteline on the N; the Tyrol and the prov. of Brescia on the E; the prov. of Cremona and of Lodi and Crima on the S; and the prov. of Milan and Corno on the W. Its greatest length from NE to SW is 72 m.; and its greatest breadth from NW to SE is 36 m. Its superficies is about 1,650 sq. m. The N. E, and W limits of this prov. are formed by the elevated branches of the Rhætian Alps, which reach even to the environs of Bergamo. The mountains, Tonal, Corno, Pizgana, and Gadina, are most remarkable for their elevation. Between these mountains, several beautiful valleys open out, as those of Camonica, Seriana, and Brimbana, which are watered by the Oglio, the Serio, and the Brimbo. These rivers, with the Adda on the SW boundary, are the chief rivers. All the northern and western part of the lake of Isco belong to this province. Numerous mineral springs occur in the district, amongst others those of Triscorre. The climate of the northern part is pure and temperate; the soil of the valleys is fertile; yet the crop of grain is hardly sufficient for six months' consumption; but the meadows and pastures, which nourish numerous flocks, afford the inhabitants a rich compensation. Game abounds in the forests, and fish in the lakes and rivers. Much iron is produced from the mines, together with abundance of whetstones. The rearing of silk-worms is the principal branch of industry. There are also manufactures of wool. The inhabitants speak the worst dialect of all Italy; but they are active and laborious. The prov. of B. comprises 372 communes, with 340,211 inhabitants in 1837. It is divided into the 18 districts of Almeno, St. Salvatore, Alzano- Maggiore, Bergamo, Brino, Capricio, Clusone, Edolo, Gandino, Dovero, Martingango, Piazza, Ponte San Pietro Romano, Sarnico, Trescorre, Traviglio, Verdello, and Zogno. The Bergamasco, as it is called, belonged to the Venetians; but was seized by the French in 1796, and formed for some years a part of the Italian republic. It was subsequently included in the kingdom of Italy, and contributed to form the departments of the Serio, and of the Adda and Oglio. Bergamo, a city of Upper Italy, the cap. of the above district, is situated between the rivers Brembo and Serio, two small tributaries of the Adda, 29 m. NE of Milan; and is built in the form of an amphitheatre, on the first rise of the Alps, on the side of a rocky hill. It is provided with walls, bastions, and ditches; and is besides protected and commanded by a castle, to which there is a covered passage from the city. The fortifications of B. are deserving of attention, having been constructed by the most eminent engineers of the 16th cent. This town contains 15 churches, 12 convents for men, and 10 for women, 4 hospitals, 6 orphan houses, a lyceum, a school of painting and architecture, and a seminario, with a public library of 45,000 vols. The cathedral contains many valuable paintings. One of the most remarkable edifices in B. is a stone building, called the Fiera, containing 540 booths, in which a great annual fair is held. The fair, which begins on the 24th of August, and lasts for a fortnight, is frequented by a vast concourse of merchants from Italy, Germany, and Switzerland; and the value of the goods disposed of at it sometimes exceeds £1,000,000, onethird of which consists of silk. The great trade of the town is in silk, of which article there were formerly exported yearly to Germany, France, and England, more than 1,200 bales, valued on an average at £150,000 sterling. The other articles of export are wine, iron-with which this city supplies the whole of Lombardy-and grindstones. The merchants of B. import fine cloths from England, France, and Holland; camblets from Holland and Flanders; and coarse cloths, spiceries and drugs, from Germany. From the Milanese and other parts of Lombardy, they receive corn,—the territory of the town not producing enough for the consumption of the inhabitants. B. is the see of a bishop, who is a suffragan of Milan, and had formerly the title of count. Its pop. is 32,000.—The date of the foundation of this city has not been precisely ascertained, but it is wellknown to have existed under the Romans. It was taken successively by Attila, the Lombards, and Charlemagne. In the 12th cent. it suffered much from the contentions between the Guelphs and Gibellines, and it fell under the dominion of Venice in 1428. Louis got possession of it in 1509, but held it only for some years. In 1796 it was taken by the French, who subsequently made it the cap. of the dep. of Serio. Among the eminent natives of B., may be mentioned the father of Tasso, John Peter Maffei, an elegant Latin writer, the Abbé Serassi, and Jerome Tiraboschi, the learned historian of Italian literature. B. has also produced several distinguished artists.
BERGAMA. See BERGHAMEH.
BERGANTES, a town of Spain, rising in Valencia, and flowing N into Aragon, where it is joined by the Guadalope, a little to the S of Calanda.
BERGANTI, or Bubistas, a mountain range of Western Greece, in the prov. of Acarnania, the Olympus Acarnaniæ of the ancients.
BERGANTINO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the Polesino, W of Rovigo. Pop. 3,000.
BERGARA. See VERGARA.
BERGE, a town of Norway, on the NW coast of Nordland, in the cant. of Lofodden. Pop. 1,200.— Also a town of Holland, in the prov. of Drenthe, S of Emmen.

BERGEDORF, a district in the territory of Hamburg, between the Elbe and the Rille, comprising 40 sq. m., with a pop. of 11,000. Its cap., of the same name, is 9 m. SE of Hamburg, on the Rille river. Pop. 2,400.
BERGEGGI, a small island of Sardinia, in the gulf of Genoa, SW of Savona.
BERGEIK, or BERG-EIJK, a town of Holland, in N Brabant, 10 m. SW of Eindhoven. Pop. 1,586.

BERGEN, or Bergenhuus, a diocese or province of Norway; bounded on the N by the prov. of Trondheim; on the E by the prov. of Aggerhuus and of Christiansand; on the S by Christiansand; and on the W by the North sea. It lies between the parallels of 59° 30' and 62° N lat.; and the meridians of 5° 30' and 8° E long. It is divided into the amts or bailiwicks of the city of B., Sondre B., Nordre B., and a part of the amt of Romsdäl, comprising the district of Sondmöer. These are subdivided into 5 fogderie, 57 pastorates, and 165 parishes. The area of the amt of Sondre or South B. is 143.23 Swedish, or 6,306 English sq. m., with a pop. in 1845 of 69,366; that of Nordre or North B., 171.26 Swedish, or 7,518 English sq. m., with a pop. in 1845 of 79,447. The pop. now stated is according to the Almanach de Gotha for 1849, in which it is given on the authority of Forssell; but in the tables published in Macgregor's Commercial Tariffs, the pop. of Lower B. in 1835 is BER stated at 85,595; and of Upper B. at 70,776 in the same year. The coast-line, like that of all the rest of Norway, is deeply indented with gulfs and fiords, the largest of which are the Vaags-fiord, which runs up, under various names, across the whole northern part of the prov., almost to the base of the Lodals Kaabe; the Dale-fiord; the Sogne-fiord, whose headwaters intersect the Sogne-field in a curious manner; and the Hardanger-fiord or Bommel-fiord, in the south. The principal river, the Leerdals-elf, descends from the Fille-field into the Sogne-fiord.—The Lang-field, Sneebraen, Sogne-field, Fille-field, and Hardanger-field, form, in succession from N to S, a huge mountain wall-separating this prov. on the E from Aggerhuus-and several summits in which attain an alt. of from 7,000 to 8,000 ft. From these mountains, various offsets shoot across the country in all directions, so that the general surface consists of deep branching gulfs, and narrow valleys, running up between lofty mountain-ridges. In such a region, arable ground is necessarily of very limited extent in proportion to the surface which may be covered with natural pasture or forests; and the principal branch of rural industry is the rearing of cattle and sheep. The pop. along the coast and the sides of the fiords are almost exclusively engaged in fishing.

BERGEN, a town of Norway, the cap. of the dio. of the same name, and of the amt of Sondre Bergenhuus, in N lat. 62° 23', E long. 5° 20'. It is built "in the shape of a hammer," partly on the NW shore of a tongue of land jutting out into the Byefiorden, and partly on a small islet. On the mainland it is enclosed by high mountains, one of which, called Atriken, rises to the height of 2,600 ft.; another, called the Lyderhorn, has an alt. of 1,200 ft. The summits of these mountains are naked masses of rock; and much of the territory in the immediate vicinity of B. has a bleak and sterile aspect. The town itself contains about 2,500 houses, which are chiefly arranged in one long winding street, from which a few shorter streets branch off. Many of these streets are so narrow, crooked, and uneven, that even small vehicles could only pass each other in them with difficulty; while the houses themselves are mostly of wood, and huddled together with little attention to symmetry of arrangement. The houses, over their white plastering, are protected against the skyey influences by coats of oil and varnish: this gives to the place a dazzling glary look. The market-place forms a good square; and the best houses, with the exception of the suburban villas, are in this part of the town. There are neither inns nor hotels in the town, because few persons visit it except on commercial pursuits, and these find board and lodging with the parties with whom they transact business. The principal manufacturing establishments in the town are tobacco-works, distilleries, and ropeworks. Ship-building also constitutes a principal branch of industry. The harbour is good and commodious, accessible generally all the year round, and defended by several forts.—The commerce of B. is very active. Its exports are herrings, lobsters, dried fish, train-oil, tar, deals, tallow, skins, and lichens. The principal article of export is fish, with which B. is supplied by the great fisheries near the Lofoden isles, the cured produce of which is brought to B. in the month of August, and by the Nordlandmen, or inhabitants of the northern provinces of Norway, who visit B. generally twice a-year, with cargoes of fish oil, salted roes, and cured fish. A very full account of these fisheries is given in the article Ber- GEN in the Penny Cyclopedia. In 1767, the exports from B. were valued at 2,758,944 rix - dollars; in 1787, they had increased to 3,939,385 rixdollars. In 1842, 1,078 vessels entered the port, of a total tonnage of 80,545 tons, and manned by 6,311 hands. Of this tonnage 40,503 tons was in Norwegian shipping; 11,787 in Danish; 6,535 in Swedish: 4,620 in Dutch; and 3,747 in British.—The pop. of B. in 1825 was 20,844, while Christiania, the cap., had a pop. in the same year of only 19,061. In 1833 its pop. was 22,839, but Christiania had gained upon it, returning a pop. of 23,121. The number of paupers in B. in 1835 was 831, being nearly one-sixth of the entire pauperism of the kingdom. The number of merchants was returned in the same year at 371; of manufacturers and artisans at 700; the clergy at 23; civil officers at 63; and military officers at 46.— Besides a college, and other schools, B. possesses a naval academy and 5 public libraries.—B. sends 4 representatives to the storthing, or senate of Norway.—B. was the residence of the Norwegian kings until the union of Calmar. In 1445, a factory was established here by the Hanseatic league. In the 17th cent. it was repeatedly ravaged by the plague; and on the 10th of May, 1702, nearly the whole town was destroyed by fire. Bishop Eric Pontoppidan, and Ludvig Holberg the historian, were natives of B.

BERGEN, a town of Limburg, in the arrond. and 30 m. N of Ruremonde. Pop. 3,384.—Also a town of Holland, in the prov. of N Holland, 3 m. NW of Alkmaar. Pop. 770. The English forces were defeated here in Sept. 1799 by the French.-Also a town of Prussia, in Pomerania, on the N coast of the island of Rugen, of which it is the cap., 15 m. NW of Stralsund. Pop. in 1837, 2,806. The circle of B. comprises the islands of Rugen, Hiddensöe, Libitz, Ummauz, Publitz, Urkevitz, and Vilin.—Also a town of Hanover, in the prov. of Luneburg, on the Dumme, 12 m. SW of Luchow. Pop. 822, many of whom are linen weavers.—Also a town of Hesse-Cassel, in the circle and 5 m. WNW of Hanau. Pop. 1,783. Good wine and fruits are produced in its neighbourhood. The French here defeated the Imperialists in April 1759.—Also a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Bavaria, 5 m. SW of Traunstein. Pop. 134. Iron-works have existed here for upwards of three centuries.—Also an islet off the W coast of the island of Sumatra.

BERGEN, a county in the NE part of the state of New Jersey, U. S. Area 350 sq. m. It is watered by Hackensack and Saddle rivers. Pop. 13,223.— Also a township in Genessee co., in the state of New York, 14 m. NW of Batavia, on Black creek.—Also a township, and the cap. of Hudson co., in New Jersey, 56 m. NE of Trenton. Pop. 5,255.

BERGEN-OP-ZOOM, a town, and very strong fortress of Holland, in the prov. of N Brabant, and arrond. of Breda. It is situated in a marshy district near the r. bank of the E Scheld, with which it communicates by the Zoom canal, 17 m. N by W of Antwerp, and 21 m. W by S of Breda. The town has a good market-place; but the streets are narrow. The church is a fine structure. It has a pop. of 6,000; and its chief trade arises from supplying the garrison of the place, and extensive potteries. B. was first surrounded by a wall in 1287; and was regularly fortified in 1442. In 1586 and 1622, it was unsuccessfully besieged by the Spaniards; but was taken by the French in 1747, who restored it in a shattered state at the peace of Aix-la-Chapelle, after which its fortifications were renewed. In 1795, the French again got possession of it; and they held it till the close of the war. The British troops under Sir Thomas Graham, made an unsuccessful attempt to take this place by storm in 1814. BERGENHUUS (NORDRE and SONDRE). BERGEN.

BERGERAC, an arrondissement, canton, and commune of France, in the dep. of La Dordogne .- BER The arrond has an area of 214,542 hectares. Pop. in 1836, 117,302; in 1846, 119,321. It comprises 13 cantons: viz., Saint-Alvère, Beaumont, B., Cadouin, Eymet, Issigeac, Laforce, Lalinde, Monpazier, Sigoulès, Velines, Villamblard, and Villefranche - de- Lonchapt.—The cant. comprises 11 com., and had a pop. of 15,098 in 1831.—The com. and town are 28 m. SSW of Perigueux, on the r. bank of the Dordogne. Pop. in 1789, 7,826; in 1836, 9,285; in 1846, 11,455. It is the seat of a Calvinist consistory, a tribunal of commerce, and a communal college. Its chief manufactures are iron, paper, leather, serges, and brandy. It has an active trade with Bourdeaux and Libourne, in wines, brandy, grain, truffles, and wood.
BERGERONNES (Les), two rivers of Lower Canada, which flow into the St. Lawrence, in Saguenay co., a few leagues below the Saguenay river.
BERG GIESHUBEL, a town of Saxony, in the Meisner circle, S of Pirna. Pop. 644.

BERGHAMEH, or BERGAMA, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Anatolia, in the sanjak of Kudavardikar, 15 m. from the sea, and 4 m. N of the river Caicus. It occupies the site of the ancient Pergamos, and presents some interesting remains of antiquity.
BERGHAUSEN, a village of Baden, in the Middle Rhine circle, 4 m. SSE of Offenburg. Pop. 998. Coal is wrought in its environs.—Also a town of Bavaria, in the Pfalz circle, S of Speier. Pop. 662.

BERGHEIM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Ribeauville. Pop. 3,518.— Also a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, 14 m. W of Cologne, on the Erft. Pop. 800.—Also a town in the principality of Waldeck, 4 m. SW of Waldeck, on the Eder. Pop. 1,400.
BERGHOLT (East), a parish in Suffolk, 6 m. SSE of Hadleigh, on the line of the Eastern Counties railway. Area 2,380 acres. Pop. 1,461.
BERGHOLT (West), a parish in Essex, 3 m. NW of Colchester. Area 2,170 acres. Pop. 822.
BERGO, a small island of Sweden, off the coast of N Bothnia, to the E of Luleä.
BERGOO. See Borgu.
BERG-REICHENSTEIN, or Unter-Reichen-
STEIN, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Prachin, on a mountain to the SW of Pisek, which formerly contained mines of gold and silver. It is 20 m. W of Prachatitz, and 36 m. WSW of Bechin.
BERGRHEINFELD, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Unter-Franken-Aschaffenburg, 4 m. W of Schweinfurt, on the Main. Pop. 850. It carries on an active trade in corn, and in white cabbage.
BERGSIO, a village of Sweden, in Helsingland, on the road from Sundsvall to Delsbo. Clarke says "there is nothing in the vale of Keswick superior to the scenery here."

BERGSTADT, or BERGSTADT-HANGENSTEIN, a market-town of Moravia, in the circle and 20 m. N of Olmutz. Considerable mines of lead and silver exist in the neighbourhood, the working of which has recently been renewed. Pop. 1,293. BERGSTADTEL, FRAUENSTADTL, OF MUTTERGOTTESBERG, a mining-town of Bohemia, in the circle of Prachin, 23 m. NW of Prachatitz. Pop. 300.

BERGUES, a canton, commune, and town of France, formerly in French Flanders, now in the dep. of Nord.—The cant., comprising 13 com., had a pop. of 15,903 in 1831.—The town stands on the river Colme, between Dunkirk and Mont-Cassel. Pop. 5,962. Since 1788, it has had free communication with the sea, through the medium of several canals, so that its maritime commerce is consider- See able. Its principal articles of trade are corn, cattle, butter, cheese, and thread. It is strongly fortified; and the surrounding country, which consists chiefly of pasture-land, can be laid under water by means of [ sluices. This place was the last town of W Flanders, which held out for the Dutch in the war of the 16th cent. The prince of Parma blockaded it with success in 1585. It was taken by the French in 1658, and secured to them by the peace of the Pyrenees.
BERGUM, a village of Holland, in West Friesland, 9 m. E of Leeuwarden, on a lake of the same name. Pop. 1,400.
BERGUN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, at the foot of Mount Adula, near Bergunerstein. Pop. 460. A route leading into the Engadine passes this v.

BERGZABERN, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Pfalz, on the Erlbach, on the borders of Lower Alsace, formerly included in the duchy of Deux Ponts. Pop. 2,800. It has a castle and two churches. Its chief trade is in wine, iron, tobacco, and pottery.
BERHAMPORE, an extensive military cantonment in Bengal, 5 m. distant from the city of Murshedabad. It is on the E bank of the Cossimbazar.
BERINBAL, a town of Lower Egypt, on the E or Damietta branch of the Nile.
BERING, a small district and town of India, in Cashmere, in N lat. 34° 18'.
BERINGEN, a town of Belgium, in Limburg, in the arrond. of Hasselt. Pop. 924.—Also a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Schaffhausen, at the foot of Randen. Pop. 1,417.
BERISLAV, a small trading town of European Russia, in the gov. of Cherson, on the Dnieper.
BERJA, a town of Spain, in Grenada, in the prov. and 20 m. W of Almeria.
BERJOU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Athis. Pop. 1,117.

BERKA, a town of Saxe-Weimar, on the Ilm, 6 m. SSW of Weimar. Pop. 1,060. There are sulphurous springs and baths here.—Also a town of Saxony, in the principality of Eisenach, on the Werra, 9 m. N of Eisenbach. Pop. 1,070.
BERKACH, a town of Saxe-Meiningen, in the prov. of Hildburghausen, NW of Romhild. Pop. 500, of whom 150 are Jews.
BERKEL. See BARKAL.

BERKEL, a river of Holland, which rises to the NE of Kösfeld, in Prussian Westphalia, and flows past Kösfeld, Stadtlohn, Borkulo, and Lochem, to the Yssel, which it joins on the r. bank, at Zutphen, after a course of 60 m.—Also a town of S Holland, 5 m. ESE of Delft. Pop. 890.

BERKELEY, a parish and market-town of Gloucestershire. The p., comprising the tythings of Alkington, Breadstone, Ham, Hamfallow, and Hinton, and the chapelry of Stone, has an area of 14,680 acres. Pop. 4,405.—The vale of B. is celebrated for the excellence of its dairy-farms, and for the production of Gloucester cheese.—The market-town of B. is 114 m. N by W of London, and 15 m. SSW of Gloucester, in the vicinity of the Gloucester and Bristol railway. It stands on an eminence about 1 m. from the Severn, on a small tributary of that river, and consists principally of four streets. Pop. in 1831, 760; in 1841, 926. It has some trade in timber, coal, malt, and cheese, which is facilitated by the vicinity of the Severn, and by the Berkeley and Gloucester canal which is navigable for vessels of 600 tons burden. This canal extends in a course nearly parallel with the Severn, from a point on the E bank of the Severn, within 21 m. of B., to Gloucester, a distance of 16 m.; and is connected with the town by a cut.—B. was the birth-place of the celebrated Dr. Edward Jenner, who, as the introducer of the practice of vaccination, obtained a European reputation. He died here of apoplexy in 1823.-Under the Saxons, B. was a place of importance. The manor, I. BER one of the largest in the kingdom, was granted by William the Conqueror to Roger de Berkeley, who built a castle here, about three-fourths of a mile from the town, on the SE side. In this castle-which Sir James Mackintosh pronounces to be "the finest specimen of the smaller castellated architecture"-Edward II. was murdered, in September 1327. Its remains consist of the keep, flanked by three semicircular towers, and a square tower of more recent date. A portion of the structure has been modernized.
BERKELEY, a parish in Somersetshire, 2 m. ENE of Frome. Area 1,880 acres. Pop. 496.
BERKESWELL, a parish in Warwickshire, 6 m. W of Coventry, on the line of the London and Birmingham railway. Area 6,360 acres. Pop. 1,504.

BERKHAMPSTEAD, a parish and market-town in Hertfordshire, 28 m. by railway NW of London, on the line of the London and Birmingham railroad. Area of p. 4,250 acres. Pop. 2,979.—The town stands in a deep valley, on a branch of the Gade. It consists of two streets, one nearly a mile in length, crossed by another at right angles. At the N end of Castle-street are the remains of the castle, which consist of detached portions of massive walls, surrounded by a moat. The female part of the population is now chiefly employed in the plaiting of straw, the manufacture of lace having declined. The trade of the place is much facilitated by the immediate neighbourhood of the London and Birmingham railroad, and the Grand Junction canal. B. was the birth-place of Cowper, the poet, in 1731. The parsonage-house in which he was born, however, was pulled down by the Rev. John Croft, rector of Berkhampstead, who also cut down the poet's favourite walnut-tree. - Dr. Stukely supposed this place to have been the Durocobrivi of the Romans. The importance of the town declined as the castle ceased to be a royal residence.
BERKHAMPSTEAD (LITTLE or East), a parish in Hertfordshire, 4½ m. SW of Hertford. Area 1,670 acres. Pop. 555.
BERKHEIM, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, SE of Biberach.
BERKHOUT, a town of Holland, in the prov. of N Holland, W of Hoorn. Pop. 1,098.

BERKLEY, a village in Bristol co., Massachusetts, U. S., 18 m. E of Providence. Pop. 886.—Also a county in Virginia, W of the Blue ridge, and separated from the state of Maryland on the N and E by the Potomac. Area 392 sq. m. Pop. 10,972.
BERKLEY'S POINT, a cape on the N coast of Egmont island, in E long. 164° 12′, S lat. 10° 42′.
BERKLEY'S SOUND, a bay on the NW coast of North America, on the E side of Quadras isles, 70 m. SE of Nootka sound.—Also a bay on the E coast of E Falkland island, in which there is good anchorage.
BERKS, a county in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., on the Schuylkill river. Area 1,020 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 64,569. Cap., Reading.

BERKSHIRE, an inland county of England, of irregular form, extending between 51° 19' and 51° 48' N lat., and 34' 30" and 1° 43' W long. Its extreme length is 48 m.; its greatest breadth 29 m.; and its circumference 208 m. It comprehends an area of 752 sq. m., or 481,280 acres; but, according to the pop. returns for 1831, 472,270 acres; while those for 1841 estimate it at 473,920 acres. On the N it is bounded by the Thames, which separates it from the counties of Gloucester, Oxford, and Buckingham; on the E by Surrey; on the S by Southamptonshire; and on the W by Wilts. Fuller compares the general outline of its figure to that of a lute; others, to a slipper or sandal. Physical features, rivers, &c.] The surface of this co. presents few abrupt or bold elevations. The most elevated tract is a series of downs,-a continuation of those in the northern part of Wiltshire. The White Horse hill, a part of this range, rises on the W boundary of the co. to an alt. of 893 ft. The forest-lands are chiefly on the E and S sides. Windsor forest is about 50 m. in circumference; but a great part of it was enclosed under an act passed in 1813. The principal river of B. is the Thames, which becomes navigable soon after touching upon it. It enters the co. at St. John's bridge, about 1 m. S of Lechlade in Gloucestershire, and skirts it during a course of more than 100 m., in which it passes the towns of Abingdon, Wallingford, Reading, Henley, Maidenhead, and Windsor. The Kennet, which is navigable from Newbury downwards, flows into the co., from Wiltshire, near Hungerford, and falls into the Thames at Reading, after a course of 25 m. in Berkshire.—The Lambourn rises in the chalk hills near the town of the same name, and falls into the Kennet near Newbury, after a course of about 12 m. -The Loddon rises near Aldershot in Hamps, enters B. in the parish of Swallowfield, and falls into the Thames near Wargrave, after a course of near 30 m., of which about 12 m. are in Berkshire. - The Ock, a stream of about 20 m. in length, flows through the vale of the White Horse, and falls into the Thames near Abingdon.—There are two lines of canal in this The Wilts and Berks canal extends from the co. Thames, a little below Abingdon, through the vale of the White Horse to the W border of the county; entering Wiltshire a little beyond Shrivenham, and joining the Kennet and Avon canal at Semmington, near Melksham. It is 52 m. in length, and 271 ft. wide at the surface. The Kennet and Avon canal crosses the lower part of the co., from the Kennet above Newbury, and enters Wiltshire a little beyond Hungerford. It is 57 m. in length, and 45 ft. wide at the surface. It was completed in 1810.—At Maidenhead the Great Western railway enters B. After passing through that town and Reading, and crossing the tum of the cultivated districts consists of chalk or gravel, with clay in different places at various depths. found in great abundance on the downs of B.; and frequently on strata with which they have no apparent connection. The most fertile district is the vale of the White Horse, which receives its name from the figure of a gigantic horse cut on the side of a hill so as to expose the white chalk below. The vale of the Kennet is next in natural fertility, and perhaps superior in cultivation. Wheat, oats, barley, beans, pease, buck-wheat, vetches, rape-seed, turnips, and potatoes, are extensively cultivated; of less extensive cultivation are onions, carrots, hops, woad, flax, asparagus, and lavender. The average rent of land is about 25s. per acre. The predominant wood is hazel; but ash and alder are not uncommon. The cattle are generally of the long-horned or common country breed. There is a strongly marked breed of sheep, called Not sheep, said to be native, but pure specimens are not common. The cart-horses are good and strong, but not tall. Pigs and poultry are numerous in the dairy-districts, and, from the proximity of London, very profitable to the farmer. The true B. breed of pigs is highly esteemed. A large quantity of malt is made in the co., of which a considerable portion is sent to the metropolis.—The climate of Berkshire is mild; the airsalubrious and pure. There are, comparatively speaking, few manufactures in this co.; its prosperity mainly depending upon the export and import of commodities through the medium of the Thames. The proportion of the pop. engaged in agriculture, in 1831, was 45.2 in 100; in trade and manufactures, 31.8. Ecclesiastical affairs.] B. is in the prov. of Canterbury; and, with the exception of two parishes, was formerly in the dio. of Salisbury. It has been recently transferred, in accordance with the recommendation of the church-commissioners, to the dio. of Oxford. It forms an archdeaconry, which contains the deaneries of Abingdon, Newbury, Reading, and Wallingford; and is divided into 147 parishes, of which 71 are rectories, 64 vicarages, and 12 perpetual curacies. In 1829 there were 81 Dissenting congregations in this county; in 1838, six of these were Roman Catholic. It possessed, in 1835, 511 daily schools, attended by 15,881 scholars; and 23 infantschools, containing 693 children; forming a total of 16,574 young persons under daily instruction. There were, besides, 225 Sunday schools, attended by 14,113 individuals. Hundreds, Rates, &c.] Berkshire is divided into 20 hundreds, the area and pop. of which, in 1831 and 1841, were as follows: Pop. in 1831. The boroughs of Reading, Wallingford, and Windsor, each returns two members to parliament; Abingdon, one. The county also contains the incorporated market-towns of Maidenhead, Newbury, and Wokingham; and the market-towns of Farringdon, Hungerford, East-Ilsley, Lambourn, and Wantage. Besides the members for the boroughs, the co. returns two knights of the shire to parliament. The number of electors in 1839-40 was 5,708; in 1842-3, 5,513. The co.-members are nominated at Abingdon, and polled for at Abingdon, Reading, Newbury, Wantage, Bracknell, Maidenhead, Great Farringdon, and East-Ilsley. B. is included in the Oxford circuit. The Lent assizes and Epiphany quarter-sessions are held at Reading; the Easter sessions at Newbury; the Summer-assizes and Hilary sessions at Abingdon; and the Michaelmas sessions alternately at Abingdon and Reading. The county-bridewell or house of correction is at Abingdon; and the county-gaol and house of correction, at Reading. The amount levied for poor-rate, county rate, &c., in B. for the year ending Lady-day in 1813 was £188,418, being at the rate of 31s. 10d. per head on the pop. in 1811. In 1834, it was £127,229; being at the rate of 17s. 6d. per head on the pop. in 1831. In 1844, it amounted to £98,331; being at the rate of 12s. 2d. per head on the pop. in 1841. Antiquities.] The churches in this county present some curious remains of Saxon and Norman architecture. There were anciently twelve religious houses in B., one of which was an alien priory, and two were preceptories of the Knights-Hospitallers. There were also three colleges, one of which is still extant in the royal chapel of St. George at Windsor; and ten hospitals, two of which still exist at Abingdon, and others at Donnington, Lambourn, and Newbury. There was a magnificent abbey at Reading, built by Henry I., of which scarcely any traces remain. Roman remains occur in various places; but the only well-ascertained Roman station is that of Spinae, the modern Speen. This co. gives the title of Earl to a branch of the family of Howard, the representative of which is the earl of Suffolk and Berkshire. Name and history.] The name of this co. has been changed into its present form from Barrocshire or Berrocshire, which is by some supposed to have been derived from a wood abounding in box, and called Barroc, or Berroc. Some—as Higden and Brompton, though perhaps fancifully—deduce its etymology from berroc, a 'bare oak,' beneath which the Britons were wont to hold their provincial meetings. "It is more probable, however," to use the language of an etymologist, "that it may have been derived from the quantity of birch wood produced in the county in former ages, the soil in general being more adapted to the growth of that wood than any other." At the time of the Roman invasion this co. was inhabited by the Bibroci and Attrebatii. Under the Saxon heptarchy it formed part of the kingdom of Wessex, and was the scene of frequent conflicts with the Danes. The castle of Wallingford was garrisoned by Brian Fitzcourt for the Empress Matilda, during the struggle between her and Stephen, and afforded her a retreat when driven from Oxford. The castles of Wallingford and Windsor were seized by John, while his brother, Richard I., was absent on an expedition to the Holy Land; and during the quarrels between John and his nobles, meetings were held for the redress of grievances alternately in Wallingford and Reading, the former of which was fortified by the king, the latter was in the hands of the barons. During the parliamentary war, Wallingford and Windsor were garrisoned, the former for the king, and the latter for the parliament; and each retained possession of these places till the conclusion of the war.

BERKSHIRE, a county in the state of Massachusetts, U.S. It lies on the W frontier of the state, and is intersected by the Green mountain range, which is here about 1,200 ft. in average elevation. It is watered by the head-branches of the Housatonic and Hoosick rivers; and the Boston and Albany railroad passes through it. Pop. 41,745. Cap. Lenox.—Also a township in Franklin co., Vermont, 22 m. NE by E of St. Alban's. Pop. 1818 .- Also a township in Tioga co., New York, 148 m. WSW of Albany. Pop. 956.—Also a township in Delaware co., Ohio, 28 m. N of Columbus. Pop. 1,407.
BERKSKOI, a town of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Tomsk, on the Obi, SW of Tomsk.
BERLAER, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Antwerp, 16 m. NE of Malines, on the Grande- Nethe. Pop. 2,925.

BERLAIMONT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, arrond. of Avesnes.— BER The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,975.— The com. and town are 8 m. NW of Avesnes, on the Sambre. Pop. 2,068.
BERLANGA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. of Soria, on the Escalote, 24 m. SW of Soria. Pop. 1,500.—Also a town in Estremadura, 70 m. SE of Badajoz.

BERLEBURG, a town of Prussian Westphalia, in the circle of Wittgenstein, 30 m. SSE of Arnsberg, at an alt. of 1,433 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1837, 2,120. There is a castle here, the residence of the prince of Sayn-Wittgenstein-Berleburg.
BERLENGAS. See BARLENGAS.

BERLICHINGEN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the Jaxt circle, 8 m. WNW of Kunzelsau, on the l. bank of the Jaxt, at an alt. of 666 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,407, of whom a considerable proportion are Jews. The ruins of the ancient castle of the celebrated Götz of B. exist here.

BERLIKUM, a town of Holland, in the prov. of N Brabant, on the Aa river, 8 m. E of Bois-le-Duc. Pop. 2,000.—Also a town in the prov. of Frieseland, 10 m. W by N of Leeuwarden. It has repute for its fine breed of horses.

BERLIN, a city of Germany, the cap. of the Prussian dominions, situated on the banks of the Spree, a considerable stream which falls into the Havel, one of the tributary rivers of the Elbe. It is in N lat. 52° 31′ 30″, E long. 13° 23′; 114 m. NNE of Leipsic; 192 m. E of Hanover; and 300 m. N by W of Vienna. Alt. above sea-level, 127 ft. Frederic II., who employed all his intervals of peace in improving the internal condition of his states, bestowed much care and expense on the embellishment of B. Before the war of 1756, he had already reared several public edifices, and entirely rebuilt the suburb called Neuvoigtland, the houses of which he gave gratuitously to the proprietors. In the dreadful period of strife which ensued, B. fell twice into the hands of the enemy. Haddik, the Austrian general, entered it in 1757, and levied 200,000 German crowns. Three years afterwards, it was seized by the combined army of Austria and Russia, who exacted from it 2,000,000 crowns, a sum which it was necessary to borrow. The debt, however, was liquidated by Frederic after the conclusion of the war, nor was any additional burden ever imposed on the inhabitants on account of it. In eight years—from 1769 to 1777 -Frederic caused to be rebuilt 149 private houses, which he presented as free gifts to their former proprietors; and during the last six years of his reign— from 1780 to 1785 inclusive—he expended 4,561,200 livres on the embellishment of his favourite city. The liberal schemes of Frederic were eagerly adopted by his successor Frederic William II.; who, besides erecting many houses which he also gave gratuitously to private individuals, constructed the gate of Brandenburg, in the style of the Propyleum of Athens; a part of the walls of the city; a stone bridge, and one of iron. The late reigning monarch, too, after the example of his predecessors, expended considerable sums in the embellishment of B. This city lies in the Middle mark of Brandenburg, and is composed of five towns: viz., Berlin Proper, Cöln, Friedrichswerder, Dorotheenstadt, and Friedrichstadt. Berlin Proper is said to owe its origin to a colony of Netherlanders, who founded this city in 1152, under the reign of Albert the Bear. The other towns were added after successive periods. In 1645, Berlin contained only 1,236 houses; in 1747, 5,513; in 1779, 6,437; in 1844, 81,298. In 1774, it contained 104,874 inhabitants; in 1798, exclusive of the garrison, according to Hoeck, 142,099; in 1803, 153,123, exclusive of the garrison, with a proportional increase of houses; in 1817, 188,485; and in 1844, according to the State Gazette, 354,000. In 1838, the number of houses was 14,200. The whole city, including its five divisions, is 12 m. in circumf.; being 4½ m. in length, and 3 m. in breadth; but within this enclosure are many gardens, and even fields. The entrance to B. is agreeable, though the country is flat and dusty except where relieved by kitchen-gardens and moist-looking vegetables. The city itselfnotwithstanding the peculiar patronage it has enjoyed under successive sovereigns-is a long, straggling, unsymmetrical, discrepant metropolis, - a jumble of magnificent buildings and ruinous houses. B., says a recent traveller, "has the air of the metropolis of a kingdom of yesterday. No Gothic churches, narrow streets, fantastic gable - ends, no historical stone and lime, -no remnants of the picturesque ages, - recall the olden time. The streets are spacious and straight, with broad margins on each side for foot-passengers; and a band of plain flag-stones on these margins makes them much more walkable than the streets of most continental towns; but these margins are divided from the spacious carriage-way in the middle by open kennels, boarded over only at the gateways of the palaces, to let the carriages cross them." Another recent visitor says: "B. appears a city built for effect; all that is beautiful is concentrated, as it were, in one focus; and, in diverging from this we are doomed to encounter a great number of glaring incongruities. The streets, broad even to dreariness, have not the advantage of trottoirs, and few of the houses exceed two stories high; add to which the small sharp stones with which the pavement is formed, renders walking sufficiently disagreeable. An Englishman, accustomed to the hive-like population of his own commercial cities, would be inclined to suppose that pestilence had been dealing, at no distant period, desolation throughout the city. This gloomy tristeness reigns triumphant over most of the streets, except when a review, or some other public occasion, attracts the population of the provinces. The architecture of the public buildings is also censurable from its tiresome uniformity, arising from a too frequent repetition of the same forms and combinations; for even granting that its Ionic porticos—the most general style—are perfect models of perfection, yet their endless repetition is wearying even to satiety." B. has 15 gates; and is surrounded by ramparts; but they are of little value as means of defence. The streets in the best part of the town are straight, and well-paved; and there are several large and handsome squares with pleasant walks. The houses of the city are in general built of fine white freestone; but in the suburbs they are almost all of wood plastered. The royal gate of the city is defended by a half-moon, and two bastions, covered with brick; it fronts the royal street, which is one of the longest and best-frequented in the city, and is full of very handsome houses, especially those of the ministersof-state. On the stone bridge over the Spree, is an equestrian statue of Frederic William the great elector, esteemed an excellent piece of workmanship. Standing on this bridge, you can see all the principal edifices and architectural adornments of B. "by turning on your heel—a sweep that brings within view the museum, the arsenal, the university, the opera, the library, the Lustgarten, the statue of Blucher, the Linden, with the Brandenburg-gate and the celebrated car of Victory in the far distance; under a sky rivalling that of Italy in clearness-for smoke is unknown-with a bright sun, and the life that noon calls into existence, the view is, perhaps, unequalled in the world. But this," adds the journalist, whose language we now employ, chiming in with the remarks already quoted, "this is the whole city; all the rest is a dreary expanse of straight, empty streets, too long and too wide for the traffic, for most of the capital was built 'according to order' from Frederick the Great, who enclosed an enormous space, and directed it to be covered with houses."-Beyond the bridge appears the royal palace, a grand and superb edifice, four stories high, with extremely magnificent rooms. The furniture of the great apartments is exceedingly rich. There is also a handsome gallery, 50 paces long, adorned with pictures and paintings by the best masters. - The Wiesse saal, or White hall, in which the United Diet holds its debates, is one of the noblest rooms in the royal palace. It is 105 ft. long, 51 ft. broad, and 41 ft. high; and is adorned with Corinthian columns of Carrara marble bearing the marble statues of the twelve princes-electors of Brandenburg. The eight provinces of the monarchy are also represented by eight colossal statues. The roof is richly painted, and the four corners are adorned with allegorical basreliefs. The main colour of the saloon is white, blended with gold. The floor is composed of ornamented wood. A marble figure of Victory, by Rauch, is one of the chief attractions of the noble room. The king's throne is placed near the middle of one of the oblong walls; seats for the ministers are ranged on each side, and, farther down, for the members of the upper house when the diet is in pleno. Opposite the throne, are the seats of the members of the eight provincial diets, each state being separate from the other. The seats are numbered, covered with dark red cloth, and provided with moveable desks. The respective members of the three states sit in rows one above the other. Each speaker addresses the diet from a species of pulpit erected for that purpose near the throne.—The royal stables stand near the palace, and front the great street. Their interior is magnificent and elegant. The mangers are of stone; and the pillars which divide the stalls, of iron adorned with the royal cipher. Over the racks are pictures representing the finest horses which the royal stud has produced; and above the stables are large rooms containing horse-furniture, and the equestrian accoutrements of Frederic I., the ornamental part of which is of gold set with diamonds.—The arsenal consists of four grand buildings, which form a court in the interior, each front having three large porticoes .- The opera house is an elegant modern edifice. It has a noble portico supported by Corinthian columns, and a pediment adorned with basso relievos and statues. The columns which support the roof throw the whole into a grand saloon. It has three galleries, and is capable of containing 2,000 persons.—Schinkel, the great architect of Berlin, has built a beautiful museum opposite the royal palace. It measures 276 ft. by 120 ft.—An iron monument, in commemoration of the victories obtained by the Prussians over the French, in the late war, is erected upon the Kreuzberg, or 'Mountain of the Cross,' at the Halle gate. This monument, which is in the Gothic style, and entirely of cast-iron, is in the shape of a pyramid, with an iron cross at the top. Including the five steps, which encompass it in an octagonal form, it measures 61 ft.; and weighs, inclusive of the statues, 2,297 cwt. 80 lb.—The finest street, the Unterder-Linden, stretches in a direct line from the square in front of the palace. It is at least half-a-mile in length, and of great width, and presents on either side the finest houses which the city contains, while five noble avenues run throughout its whole length. Among the public institutions of B. are the academies of painting, and of arts and sciences; the observatory, which is well-furnished with astronomical and mathematical instruments; the royal cabinet of medals, antiquities, and natural curiosities; the chemical laboratory, with its furnaces and metals; the anatomical theatre; and the royal library, which contains 150,000 volumes, and is considered one of the most complete in Germany. Berlin has 37 churches, 2 hospitals for invalids, a military academy, 4 colleges, 5 gymnasiums, and upwards of 250 places of education under the superintendence of nearly 1,000 male and female teachers. It also possesses a botanical garden, a museum established in 1820, and several charitable and humane institutions. A most important institution is the Charity, which admits above 5,000 sick persons, and is one of the most celebrated schools for young physicians and surgeons. There are also a missionary and a Bible society, and several almshouses. It has been calculated that 12,000 individuals are supported at B. on the charity of their fellow-citizens.—The university of B. was founded in 1809; and though a comparatively young establishment, holds a very high rank among the educational institutions of Europe. In 1826 it was attended by 1,640 students, of whom 400 were foreigners. The average number of students is now reckoned at 1,800.—The pleasures of B. are nearly the same as of other large towns. The royal theatre and opera house are much frequented, and the performers and singers are reckoned among the best in Germany. The national taste for music is much cherished, and is supported by extensive establishments. B. is considered as the centre of civilization for the north of Germany. "There are one or two other peculiarities attributed to the North Germans, and particularly the people of B., which should not be quite passed without notice. They are of mixed origin-Germans, Sclavonians, Poles, French, and Jews; and the South Germans will not allow them the name of true Germans, they say they are Prussians. They are also charged with a greater degree of stiffness and coldness of manner than the South Germans. We could observe nothing of the sort amongst the educated classes. The porters of B. are a peculiar race, celebrated all over Germany. They are called Eckensteher, or 'Corner-standers,' from their habit of collecting in groups at public corners. They have a badge on the arm, and are readily known by their original humour. They bandy sharp words in their peculiar dialect with great effect. They are a most un-German sort of fellows—the Irish of Berlin. They have a deal of sly cunning and drollery; a dry manner; will have the last word; and are sure to turn the laugh against their antagonists, be they high or low, educated or uneducated." [Howitt's Social Life of Germany.] The manufactures of B. are important. They chiefly consist of fine woollen goods, carpets, cottons, silk-stuffs, lace, porcelain, fine cast-iron and bronze ware, cabinet-work, straw-hats, paper, and gunpowder. The Spree is navigable for boats drawing 9 ft. of water, and is connected by canals with the Elbe on the one hand, and with the Oder on the other; while five important railway lines meet at B. in a common centre, uniting it with Stettin, at the mouth of the Oder, on the N; with Frankfort, on the same river, on the E; with Roslau, on the Elbe, and with Bernburg and with Halle, on its confluent the Saale, on the S; and from Halle, with Leipzig and Dresden, towards the E; with Brandenburg, on the Havel, and with Magdeburg, on the Elbe, on the SW; and with Wittenberg and Hamburg on the W.-The city revenues in 1844 amounted to 1,506,355 rix-dollars; the expenditure to 1,442,085 rix-dollars. The rental of the inhabited houses in 1844 was 1,946,317 dollars. During the 15 years from 1769 to 1782, M. Berguelin found the average temperature of B. to be 49°.

BERLIN, a township in Coos co., in the state of New Hampshire, U. S., 111 m. N of Concord. Pop. BER H 116.—Also a township in Oxford co., in the state of Maine, 45 m. NW of Augusta. Pop. 442,-Also a township in Washington co., Vermont, 4 m. S of Montpelier. Pop. 1,598.—Also a township in Worcester co., Massachusetts, 15 m. NE of Worcester. Pop. 763.—Also a township in Hartford co., Connecticut, 10 m. S by W of Hartford. Pop. 3,411.—Also a township in Reusselaer co., New York, 26 m. E of Albany. Pop. 1,794.—Also a township in Wayne co., Pennsylvania. Pop. 488. - Also a village in Somerset co., Pennsylvania, 143 m. W by S of Harrisburg. Pop. 524.—Also a township in Milwaukie co., Wisconsin. Pop. 200.-Also a township in St. Clair co., Michigan. Pop. 255.—Also a township in Holmes co., Ohio, 95 m. NE of Columbia. Pop. 1,149.—Also a township in Knox co., Ohio. Pop. 1,091.—Also a township in Trumbull co., Ohio, 18 m. SW of Warren. Pop. 1,283.-Also a village in the township of Waterloo, in Upper Canada, 9 m. from Galt. Pop. 400.
BERLINCHEN, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, 18 m. ENE of Soldin, on a lake of the same name, also called the lake of Plöne. Pop. 1,837. Tobacco, paper, and cloth, are manufactured here.
BERLINGEN, or BERNANG, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Thurgau, 2 m. ENE of Stockhorn, on the Unter-see. Pop. 744.
BERLIZA, a town of Wallachia, in the district of Jalomitza, SW of Braila.
BERMAN, a small Kurd village, a few hours N of Mardin, finely situated in a fertile valley.
BERMATINGEN, a town of Baden, in the Lake circle, NE of Mörsburg. Pop. 534.
BERMEJA, an island in the gulf of Mexico, 40 leagues N by W of the island of Arenas, in N lat. 22° 35', W long. 91° 30'.

BERMEJO, a large river rising in the mountains of Tarija, in Peru, uniting in its descent with the Lavagen, or Grande de Jujuy, at Las Juntas, a few leagues S of Oran, and flowing more or less in a SE direction till it joins the Parana, nearly in front of Corrientes, after traversing the Great Chaco plain, on the N side, and the plain of Manzo, on the S side. Were the B. made navigable—which might be done at a comparatively small expense-Corrientes would command an excellent line of communication with Upper Peru.
BERMEJO (Rio), a stream of Buenos Ayres, in the prov. of La Rioja, rising in the Andes, and flowing, it is supposed, into the lake of Guanacache.
BERMELLAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 50 m. W of Salamanca.

BERMEO, a small port of Spain, in Biscay, 20 m. NE of Bilbao, near the embouchure of a small river of the same name. Pop. 4,250. It has a few fishing and coasting vessels; but the harbour is nearly dry at low water.
BERMERAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Solesme. Pop. 1,220.
BERMERSLEY, or Bemersley, a township in the p. of Norton-in-the-Moors, 6 m. NNE of Newcastle-under-Lyne. Pop. 211.
BERMILLO-DE-SAYAGO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 20 m. SW of Zamora.

BERMONDSEY, a parish and town in Surrey, adjoining the borough of Southwark, and 13 m. SE of St. Paul's. Area of p. 620 acres. Pop. 34,947. The various branches of trade connected with shipbuilding are extensively followed here, and there are about 60 leather-dressers and tanners' yards.

BERMUDAS, or Somers' Islands, a cluster of small islands in the Atlantic ocean, nearly in the latitude of Madeira, in number about 400, but for the most part so small and barren that they have neither inhabitants nor name. They were discovered by Juan Bermudas, a Spaniard, in 1522. The other name, Somers' islands, they received from Sir George Somers, an Englishman, who was wrecked on them in 1609, and who established the first settlement upon them. This group, situated half-way between Nova Scotia and the Antilles, belongs to the former government, and serves as a summer-station for some of the ships that winter at Halifax, and as a rendezvous for the steamers of the Royal Mail Steam packet company. The archipelago rises from a shoal 23 m. in length, and 13 m. broad; and extends in a NE and SW direction. The highest land, Tibbs' hill, at the S extremity of Bermuda Proper, is only 180 ft. in alt. Their surface probably does not exceed 20 sq. m. They are distant from Cape Hatteras in N Carolina 580 m.; and from Atwood's kays, in the Bahamas, 645 m. The islands are protected from the violence of the ocean by a girdle of reefs. Captain Vetch considers them of coral formation. From a distance they have the appearance of low sterile hills, at the basis of which the ocean is dashed into white foam. Their coast is fronted by a singular chain of rocks, about pistol-shot distant, and mostly covered at low water, inside of which there is deep water. The largest of these islands, Bermuda Proper, or Long island (a), is 20 m. long, and 2 m. in breadth. The next in size are St. George's (b), St. David's, Cooper, Ireland (c), Somerset (d), Bird Island, and None-such. On St. George's, which is only 3 m. in length, and a 1 m. in breadth, there is a town containing 500 houses; the two following have some villages; throughout the others there are only some scattered farms. A breakwater and other extensive works have been formed on Ireland island, the most westerly of the group. The island of St. George, at the E end of the chain, has different ports, and two castles; but the whole group is so completely hemmed in with rocks that it is only with caution a vessel of 10 tons burden can enter the roads. The water, however, is so clear as to enable pilots to make their way with comparative facility through the reefs. Miles Soil and productions.] These islands are mostly mountainous and barren. The soil is of three kinds; a light sandy calcareous soil; a deep red soil, also calcareous; and a highly productive peat soil. According to the information procured by Edwards, the Bermudas then contained from 10,000 to 12,000 acres of poor land, of which 9 parts in 10 were either uncultivated, or reserved in wood for the supplying of timber for building small ships, sloops, and shallops, this being the principal employment of the inhabitants. Of the land in cultivation no part was appropriated to any other purpose than that of raising Indian corn, and esculent roots and vegetables, until 1785, when the growth of cotton was attempted, but with no great success, there not being more than 200 acres laid out for this sort of cultivation. Since Edwards wrote, however, the almost barren and rugged hills have been brought into profitable cultivation. The land is well-suited to the growth of wheat, barley, and oats; and there are two harvests of Indian corn in the year, one in July, the other in December. Onions are extensively reared; and the average produce of this root here is not less than 24,000 lbs. per acre. The trees are principally the cedar and palmetto. The former is much esteemed for its fragrance, its durability, and beauty, and for the facility with which it is wrought. The palmetto, a species of wild palm, is not less common nor less useful. Its fruit resembles a plum in colour, form, and size; the wood serves for building; and the leaves, which are of an amazing length, are used to cover houses, and also manufactured into a plait for hats. Besides these, among the forest-trees are the ash, the oak, the mahogany, the caoutchouc, and the green heart; and of fruit-trees there are bananas, oranges, olives, laurels, pears, &c. The coffee-plant is still left to grow wild under the cedar-trees. Fish abound upon the coast, and are of great variety and excellence. Among the insects, the spider is remarkable for its large size; but its beautiful colours diminish the disgust it inspires. Its web is in colour and substance a perfect raw silk; and, running from tree to tree, small birds are sometimes so entangled as hardly to be able to escape. Climate.] There are few wells and no fresh water streams in these islands. That which falls from the clouds is kept in large cisterns in order to supply the inhabitants and ships-of-war. The air is considered pure and wholesome. Situated between the 32d and 33d parallels, and at about an equal distance from the West India islands, and British North America, the B. enjoy a delightful climate. The temperature is so genial that a perpetual spring prevails, except during the middle of summer, when the heats are oppressive. Winter is hardly perceptible here, and resembles the early part of an English October, the mean temperature being 60°. In winter, the seeds of Europe germinate; in summer, those of the tropics. The fields and trees are clad in eternal green; the birds sing and breed without intermission; and so salubrious is the air that invalids frequently recover their health on these islands. But these advantages are counterbalanced by frequent tempests of thunder and lightning, together with hurricanes which are at times tremendous. The 'Bermuda squalls,' as they are called, are the dread of navigators. The dew point usually ranges high. The prevailing wind is from SW. Population.] The free population of the B. in 1832, was 4,181 whites; 1,068 free blacks and people of colour; 44 strangers; and 4,149 slaves. In 1840, the pop. amounted to 8,724. Shipping and trade.] In 1832, 155 ships entered inwards, and 163 outwards, at the B. Of these onehalf belonged to the British colonies. The total value of imports was £102,742; of exports £13,784. In 1840 the imports amounted to £130,800; exports £32,231. In 1844 the imports amounted to £137,849; the exports to £22,653. In 1845 the total value of imports was £140,015; in 1846, £134,189. In 1845, the total value of exports was £21,245; in 1846, £18,592. The imports from Great Britain were worth £47,707 in 1845, and £52,079 in 1846; those from the United States were worth £72,995 in 1845, and £65,762 in 1846. The exports to Great Britain were valued at £7,293 in 1845, and £2,943 in 1846; in the United States at £3,532 in 1845, and £5,107 in 1846. In 1846 the arrowroot produced was estimated to be worth £8,084; in 1845, only £4,115. The potatoes were valued at £1,036 in 1846, whereas they had been at £1,845 in 1845. In onions too there was a falling off. Of whale oil there was 869 gallons, worth £129 in 1846, against nil in 1845. The main dependence of the colony is upon the naval and military establishments which have been formed here, and on the ship-yards, and salt-works. Government.] The B. are under a governor appointed by the Crown, and assisted by a legislative council consisting of an uncertain number appointed by the governor and approved by the Crown; and an assembly of 36 representatives, 4 of whom are elected by each parish by electors having an annual income of £30 from landed property. The judiciary is vested in a court of chancery, formed by the governor and his privy council; a court of general assize, for the trial of all crimes as well as civil disputes, presided over by a chief justice, and two puisne judges; a court of vice-admiralty, and a court of quartersessions.—The islands are divided into 9 parishes, in each of which is a church. The bishop of Nova Scotia is their superintendent. There are also 5 dissenting chapels. History.] The B. were, as already noticed, discovered in 1522 by Juan Bermudas, a Spaniard, who merely saw them at a distance, and gave them his name; but who did not land upon them, or acquire any information concerning them. About the same time, Henry May, an Englishman, was shipwrecked upon one of the largest. May returned to Europe, in a vessel of cedar wood, which he had found means to build there by the help of materials collected from the wreck of his own ship; and was the first who published any account of the B. They were found, at their first discovery, to be entirely uninhabited; and though, in 1572, the king of Spain made a grant of them to one of his subjects, no settlement was formed upon any of them by the Spaniards. In 1609, Sir George Somers, Sir Thomas Gates, and Captain Newport, on their way to Virginia as deputy governors, were cast upon the Bermudas; and, disagreeing among themselves, each of them built a ship of the cedar growing upon the islands, in which they severally arrived at Virginia. Sir George Somers or Summers having returned to these islands in quest of provisions for the colony in Virginia, died there soon after his arrival. His companions and crew, instead of returning to Virginia, sailed to England; and made so favourable a report of the beauty and fertility of the B., that the Virginia company—who claimed the property—sold them to a body of 120 purchasers, and James 1. granted them a charter. In 1612, this association fitted out a vessel for the B., with 60 planters on board, under the direction of a Mr. Richard Moore. In the mean time, three English seamen who had deserted from Sir George Somers, and remained in St. George's island, had collected a considerable quantity of ambergris which they found on the coast; and were preparing to convey their valuable cargo in an open boat to Virginia or Newfoundland; when Moore arrived from England, and seized and sold the ambergris as the property of the company. This great -though scarcely just acquisition of wealth-gave new spirit to the adventurers; and, under the good conduct of Moore, the island was soon fortified with eight or nine block-houses, the town of St. George planned as it now stands, and considerable returns made to the proprietors in England in drugs, ambergris, cedar, tobacco, and other commodities. In 1616, Moore was succeeded by Captain Daniel Tucker, who followed out the beneficial plans of his predecessor, kept the planters under the strictest discipline, and greatly encouraged the culture of tobacco. In 1619, Tucker was succeeded by Captain Butler, who brought with him 500 new settlers; raised a monument to the memory of Sir George Somers; and established a new constitution of government, resembling, as nearly as possible, that of the mother-country. There were now above 3,000 English residing in the B.; many of the first nobility had purchased plantations, and it became fashionable in England to visit these islands from motives of curiosity, health, or amusement. Many royalists took refuge in the B. during the usurpation of Cromwell; and, in 1643, the poet Waller, after his condemnation by the parliament, spent several months in these islands, which he has described in his poems as enjoying a perpetual spring, and as furnishing the most delightful residence in creation. Deceived by such flattering accounts of the serenity of the climate, and the manners of the inhabitants, the benevolent and ingenious Bishop Berkeley made a generous, but unsuccessful attempt in 1725, to establish a college in the B., for the joint purpose of propagating Christianity among the American Indians, and of facilitating the education of the British youth in that country. These islands, during the last half of the 18th century, increased very little either in value or in population. The navigation in their neighbourhood was dangerous; their situation was not at that time favourable for trade; and they were found peculiarly deficient in many of those advantages which they were at first supposed to possess. It has been alleged even, that, in many respects, they have suffered a gradual deterioration; and particularly, that from the cutting down of the large cedar-forests which once covered the islands, and sheltered them from the violence of the N winds, and protected the growth of the more delicate plants, their climate has actually become less temperate, and their soil less productive. This, however, is certainly not the case; and the English government has of late years attached considerable importance to these islands as a naval depot. Authorities.] May's account of the B. in Hak- BER luyt's collection.—Edward's History of the W Indies. -Raynal.-Parliamentary Papers.-Cotter's Sketches of Bermuda.—Columbian Navigator.—Captain Hurd's Survey.
BERMUDAS, an island lying off the coast of Cape colony in S Africa, to the N of Saldanha bay, in S lat. 33° 4', E long. 17° 54'.
BERMUL, a village of Afghanistan, 70 m. SE of Ghizni, in N lat. 32° 50'.

BERN, the most extensive of the twenty-two cantons of Switzerland, situated between 46° 20' and 47° 29' N lat., and 6° 53' and 8° 27' E long. Its greatest length from N to S is 75 m.; and its greatest breadth 52 m.; but its outline is very irregular. Its square surface, according to Schoch, is 3,720 sq. m. Guinand estimates it at 327 sq. leagues of 25 to a degree. Its present boundaries to the W are the cantons of Vaud, Friburg, and Neufchatel; France on the NW and N; on the NE the cantons of Bale and Soleure; to the E those of Aargau, Lucern, Unterwalden, and Uri; and to the S the Bernese Alps, which separate it from the Valais, and present the highest summits of the Alps in Switzerland, such as the Finsteraarhorn, the Jungfrau, the Monk, the Schreckhorn, the Eiger, the Wetterhorn, the Blumlis Alp, the Altels, the Doldenhorn, and others. This prodigious chain likewise comprehends the most extensive glaciers in Switzerland; and projects into this canton a number of branches, which form the large and beautiful valleys of the Simmen, the Lauterbrunnen, the Grindelwald, and the Hasli. The principal rivers are the Aar, and its tributaries the Kander which is augmented by the Simmen, the Sarine, and the Emmen. Near the centre of the canton the surface is generally level; but the northern part beyond Bienne is covered by the Jura system, the numerous valleys of which leave a free passage for the waters of the Doubs, the Birs, the Sus, the Theile, and many other rivers. The lakes of Brienz, of Thun, and of Bienne, are the largest in the cant. The two first, traversed by the Aar, are in the S part; the last is on the western frontier, and receives the waters of the lake of Neufchatel by the Theile. The mountains nearest the frontier of the Valais are of primitive formation; the others are limestone, and their horizontal strata rest on gneiss, granite, and slate. Mines of iron, lead, and copper, are wrought; and in the sands of many of the streamlets and rivers particles of gold are found. Mineral springs are numerous. The most frequented are those of Weissenburg, the Gurnighel, and the Blumenstein. In the mountainous parts to the S of the canton the winters are long, and the summers short. Near the lake of Bienne the temperature is very mild. The plains between the Aar on the N, and Bern, are fertile in grain, but the grain raised is not at all sufficient for the consumption. Potatoes, flax, and hemp, are reared; and the vine succeeds throughout the whole cant.—The chamois, the bear, the wild boar, the roebuck, and the bouquetin, formerly roamed in the mountains and forests of B., but they are now rarely seen. Game abounds; and horses, cattle, goats, and sheep are reared. The wool of the sheep is coarse. The manufactures consist of cloths, cotton stuffs, woollens, and silks; leather, soap, ironwares, and clockwork. The transit commerce, favoured by the navigable rivers and good roads, is important. The exports, which are considerable, consist of cattle, leather, candles, cheese, wines, kirchwasser, cloths, cottons, muslins, cravats, printed handkerchiefs, stockings, bonnets, and silk ribands. The principal imports are salt, colonial articles, wines, grain, tobacco, unwrought iron, copper, steel, cotton, wool, silk, flax, and hemp. The cant. is divided into 27 amtsbezirk, or bailiwicks: viz., Aarberg, Aarwangen, Bern, Berthoud, Buren, Cerlier, Courtelary, Delemont, Fraubrunnen, Frutigen, Gessenay, Interlaken, Konolfingen, Laupen, Moutier, Nidau, Oberhasli, Porentrui, Schwarzenburg, Seftigen, Seignelegier, Signau, Simmenthal (Bas), Simmenthal (Haut), Thun, Trachselwald, and Wangen. These bailiwicks are divided into 175 Reformed and 70 Catholic parishes, which in 1818 contained 337,984 inhabitants, of which 297,620 were of the Reformed church, 900 were Anabaptists, and 39,464 Catholics. In 1837, the pop. was returned at 407,913; and the number of inhabited houses was returned in 1835 at 56,432. The canton furnishes a contingent of 5,824 men to the Confederation; and pays a contribution of 104,080 Swiss or 156,120 French francs. According to the aristocratic constitution of 1814, the sovereign power was exercised by a bailiff, and the great and lesser councils of the city and republic of B., consisting of 200 members chosen from the city of B., and 99 from the towns and the country. The former were chosen from the citizens over 29 years old, by an elective assembly composed of the members of the lesser council, and a committee of the great. The 99 members from the towns and country were chosen partly from the towns, by the municipal authorities; partly from each of the 22 districts, into which the country was divided, by elective assemblies; and partly by the great council. Two bailiffs presided in turn, each for the space of a year, in the great and lesser councils. The former council had the legislative, the latter the executive power. The latter consisted of the two bailiffs, 23 members, and 2 secretaries; and was chosen by the former from among its own members. The constitution was altogether too aristocratic, with its 200 representatives named by the city of B. alone, and that too by an electoral commission composed of the executive and 16 members of the great council. In 1831, therefore, it was revised; and a thoroughly representative constitution adopted, according to which the Grosse rath, or Grand council, is comprised of 240 members, of whom 200 are elected every six years by the electoral assemblies, and the remaining 40 are chosen by the council itself. This council exercises not only the legislative power, but also the higher functions of the executive, and nominates the deputies to the federal diet.—The Regierungs rath, or council of regency, which is intrusted with the ordinary executive functions, is composed of an avoyer, or president, and 16 members, all of whom are selected from and nominated by the Grand council. The Reformed religion embraced by the canton in 1529, is recognised as dominant by the constitution. The Reformed clergy are composed of 5 ministers of the city of Bern, of which the first is head of all the clergy and of 83 cures. The 70 Catholic parishes depend in spirituals on the bishop of Bale. History.] Cuno von Bubenberg, in the 12th century, enclosed the small place Bern, in the vicinity of the fortress of Nydeck, with a moat and walls, and the duke of Zähringen, to whom Nydeck belonged, gave the new city laws. Its population was much increased in the 13th century. The lower nobility of the adjacent country fled to it for protection against the oppressions of the higher, and were joined by the country people, and particularly by the citizens of Friburg and Zurich. The emperor Frederic II. declared it a free city of the empire, in 1218, and confirmed its privileges by a charter, which is still preserved in the archives. In 1288, B. was besieged by Rodolph of Hapsburg, but not taken; and, in 1291, the citizens of B., under Ulrich Von Bubenberg, made war against their own nobility, commanded by Ulrich von Erlach. B. now became an asylum for all those who suffered under the oppression of the nobles of Austria, and rose to a height of power that excited the envy of other cities, as well as of its own nobility. The latter, therefore, entered into an alliance with the hostile cities, for the purpose of destroying it. Their army, consisting of 18,000 men, headed by 700 of the higher nobility, with 1,200 knights, was totally vanquished at Laupen, June 21, 1339, by the citizens of B., led by Rodolph von Erlach, though these were only one third of their number. After this victory, the city BER continued to increase, and, in 1353, entered into the perpetual league of the Helvetic confederacy, in which it held a rank inferior only to Zurich. Until the close of that century, B. enlarged its dominions, partly by purchase, and partly by conquest. In 1405, the greater part of the city was destroyed by fire, but was afterwards regularly rebuilt. The long wars with Austria, Milan, Burgundy and Savoy soon after broke out, from all which the confederacy came off victorious, and in which B. conquered Asrgau. In 1528, the citizens of B. embraced the cause of the Reformation. In the subsequent war with the duke of Savoy, they conquered the Pays de Vaud. The countries gained by conquest were governed by bailiffs, who resided in mountain castles. From that time to March 5, 1798, the prosperity and wealth of B. was constantly increasing, as may be clearly perceived from the large sums spent for the public administration. At that time, the canton contained over 5,000 square miles, and about 380,000 inhabitants. Upon the day above-mentioned, 30,000 French troops marched against B. It was again an Erlach who led 18,000 citizens of B., together with 8,000 auxiliary troops of the confederate cantons, into the field; but the memory of Morgarten, of Laupen and Murten, no longer inspired them to victory; the troops of the confederates, on their retreat, slew their own commander. B for the first time, opened its gates to an enemy, and lost about half of its possessions. The northern part was united with the present canton of Aargau, and out of the south-western (Pays de Vaud) the present canton of Vaud was formed. By the decrees of the congress at Vienna, however, the greater part of the bishopric of Bale was joined to the canton of B.

BERN, the cap. of the above canton, is situated on the banks of the river Aar, which almost encircles the town, at an alt. of 1,709 ft. above sea-level, in N lat. 46° 57′ 15", E long. 7° 26' 20". The principal streets are long, broad, and gently curved. The houses are nearly uniform, and of the same height; and are built upon arcades, which afford, even in the worst weather, a dry and sheltered pavement for foot-passengers. The streets are kept remarkably clean; and the branch of the Aar which traverses the town, supplies several fountains, which contribute to the adornment of the town, as well as to the comfort of the inhabitants.—The cathedral is a beautiful Gothic building, the cloister of which is particularly admired. It was erected in 1421 by the same architect who built the minster at Strasburg, and stands upon a fine terrace commanding a charming view of the adjacent country.—The arsenal formerly contained 60,000 stands of arms, and several trophies of Swiss valour.—The public library contains about 35,000 volumes, besides a curious collection of antiquities and medals. It possesses also a chart in relief of a part of Switzerland, a view in relief of the salt mines and glaciers of Bevieux and Aigle. executed in wood by M. Exchaquet; a collection of curiosities from Otaheite; a cabinet of Swiss medals, and a cabinet of minerals.—Besides these public buildings, there are several hospitals, an almshouse, a house-of-correction built at the suggestion of Howard, and a building for public amusements. The principal literary and scientific establishments of B. are, the university founded in 1834, with 41 professors; the lyceum, with 11 professors; the gymnasium; the school of painting and design; and several learned and scientific associations, amongst which are the Œconomical society, the society of physics and natural history, and the society of medicine, &c. There are few manufactures carried on in B. The principal are woollens, linens, cottons, silks, and delft ware.- The pop. of B. in 1825 was 17,552; in 1837, 22,422, The country round B. is highly cultivated, presenting luxuriant woods, orchards, vineyards, and corn fields. The most striking feature in the landscape is the N range of the Swiss Alps, which is seen from B. along a line of about 60 m., with its snowy summits towering from 10,000 to 12,000 ft. above the surrounding country. The sublimity of the view, in the evening, when all these colossal masses, resplendent with the rosy tint of the setting sun, resemble pyramids of ruby, baffles description. The mean annual temp. at B. is 45° 12'; that of winter 29° 37'; of spring, 45° 88'; of summer, 58° 78'; of autumn, 46° 44'.
BERNADIZ, a town of Austria, in Bohemia, SW of Tabor.

BERNAL (LA), a singular mountain in Mexico, near the route from Tampico to San Luis Potosi. Its base stretches about 4 m., and rises gradually to the centre, whence an immense cluster of naked rocks rise to an alt. of about 2,000 ft. above the level of the plain, while not another rock or mountain is to be seen within 30 m. of it. The subjoined sketch of the B. was taken by Captain Lyon from a point about 7 m. distant from its centre.
BERNALDA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 20 m. SSE of Matera, on the Basiento river. Pop. 4,812.
BERNARD, a township in Somerset co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S. Pop. 2,059.

BERNARD (SAN), a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceara, on the river Russas, a tributary of the Jaguaribe, 25 m. S of Aracati. Pop. of town and district 6,000, chiefly of Indian blood.—Also a village in the prov. of Maranhao, on the l. bank of the Parnahiba, 15 m. below Brejo.—Also a river of Texas, flowing into the gulf of Mexico in N lat. 28° 51', W long. 95° 49'.
BERNARD-CASTLE. See BARNARD-CASTLE.

BERNARD (GREAT SAINT), a mountain, or rather a mountain-pass, of the Pennine Alps, on the boundary of the Lower Valais, and of the prov. of Aosta, in N lat. 45° 51', E long. 7° 25'. A route, rugged, and very dangerous in spring on account of the avalanches, crosses this mountain, and leads from Martigny to Aosta. Nearly at the most elevated part of the pass, -1,246 toises according to Pictet, 1,257 toises according to Saussure, and 1,280 toises or 8,200 ft. according to Daubuisson, above sea-level,— is the celebrated hospice of Saint B., on the border of a small lake whose waters run towards the southern side. It is unquestionably a very elevated abode for human beings; but is not, as frequently affirmed, the most elevated habitation in the Old world, for there are many spots in the stupendous Himalaya of more than double this elevation, inhabited by human beings. It is environed by a number of peaks, amongst which are many considerable glaciers.
