Amongst these peaks are observed, to the NE the Vilan, the most elevated point of the Great Saint B., 1,780 toises or 11,400 ft., and to the WNW the Pain de Sucre, and another peak of 1,460 toises or 9,490 ft. The summit of the Chinalette, 1,408 toises or 9,023 ft., is to the NW; and in the same direction, but much nearer the hospice, is the point of the Dronaz, 1,410 toises or 9,036 ft. in alt. From the foot of these peaks, or from the glaciers, springs the Drance, which flows to the Rhone. The southern versant gives birth to some affluents of the Buttier, which enters the Doire near Aosta.—The hospice, founded in 962 by Saint Bernard of Menthon, is served by the religious of the Augustinian order, who are bound to lodge and support gratuitously all who pass the Great Saint Bernard. During the coldest months the therm. in the vicinity of the convent often falls to 20° and 22° below zero: even during summer it freezes almost every morning; and the monks do not enjoy a serene sky above ten or twelve times a-year. Notwithstanding every exertion on the part of the monks, few years pass in which some lives are not lost during the in diam.; and the stones are very large, and in gen- BER snow-storms of winter on this mountain. The bodies are placed in a chapel to the E of the convent, and such is the effect of the intense cold, that they may often be recognised at the expiry of two or three years, after which they become quite dried up, and resemble so many mummies. There is a small lake, 4 of a league in circumference, on the mountain, which is frozen nine months in the year, and is destitute of fish. In the garden of the convent only a few cabbages, salads, and roots are reared. Notwithstanding the inconveniences presented by the Grand Saint Bernard route, it is annually crossed by 7,000 or 8,000 persons; and many hundreds are sometimes found collected in the convent. This mountain is composed of alternate beds of gneiss, micaceous schist, primitive limestone, and quartz. It presents many rare plants. On the summit, at a small distance from the site of the convent, a temple stood in former ages, in which was the statue of a deity to whom the Romans gave the name of Jupiter Pennium, an epithet derived apparently from the Celtic penn or ben, and from which the name of the Pennines has been given to this portion of the Alps. The Romans also denominated the Saint Bernard Mons Jovis, whence is derived that of Mount Joux, which this mountain bore in the 10th cent., before it received the name of the founder of the convent. The Italians still call it Monte Jove, and the remains of the temple of Jupiter are still traceable on a plateau which preserves the name of the plain of Jupiter, to the W of the hospice. Many antiquities have been found on the Saint Bernard; and upwards of 500 bronze medals of the Roman emperors. From the time of Augustus, the Roman legions were wont to cross the Saint Bernard into Helvetia and Gaul. An army of Lombards passed it in 547; and other armies crossed it in the time of Charlemagne. From 1798 to 1800, upwards of 150,000 French crossed it. In 1799 the Austrians and the French engaged each other near the convent, during a whole day; the latter remained masters of the field of battle. In 1800, the French army of reserve, 30,000 strong, and commanded by Bonaparte in person, scaled the summit of the pass, with its cavalry and artillery. The remains of General Dessaix repose in the church of the convent, where a monument to his memory was erected in 1805.

BERNARD (LITTLE SAINT), a mountain of the Graian Alps, in the Sardinian states, 9 m. S of Mont Blanc, on the boundary of the duchy of Savoy, and the prov. of Aosta. The pass across this mountain, leading from the valley of the Isere to that of the Dore, is the most commodious and easy of all those in the chain of the Alps: yet, strange to say, this route is very much neglected. On the summit of the pass, at an alt. of 1,125 toises or 7,194 ft. above sea-level, is a hospice, near which are the two small lakes of Vernei and Longet. From the former of these, in the centre of the plain, on the summit of the pass—a plain 2\frac{1}{2} m. long—the Doire takes its rise. At Pont Serrant, 5 m. by the road from the Saint Bernard, this river is joined by a stream descending from the glacier of the Oratory, through a very deep and very formidable ravine; and at La Tuille, 475 toises or 3,246 ft. below the hospice, it is again joined by the Baltique, coming NW from the Ruitor, a very high snowy mountain to the SE, which forms part of the central chain of the Alps. At this junction the Doire becomes a stream of consequence, and is called the Dorëa Baltia, or the Doria Major. About 3 of a mile NE of the hospice is the Columna Jovis, or 'Column of Jove,' a broken pillar of reddish granite, about 20 ft. high, and 7 ft. in circumference; and 300 yards to the NE of this is a large circle of stones, exactly resembling a Druidical circle. It is 80 yards eral so deeply sunk in the ground, as not to be more than 2 or 3 ft. above it. This is called 'the Circle of Hannibal;' and the tradition of the country is, that that warrior held a council of war within it. That this pass was the one crossed by Hannibal there can be no reasonable doubt, after an attentive perusal of Polybius, and a comparison of the localities with his account. It is the very best passage he could have taken; and the plain on the summit is sufficiently ample to have contained his whole army, which halted here two days. The plain is well-sheltered; and during the summer there is little or no snow on it, though the high mountains on both sides, as the Aiguille de Belle Tosse, the Mountain of the Column, and Mount Cramont, on the W side, and the Valaisan and the Ruitor, on the E side, are always covered with it. Snow begins to fall here in September; and the inhabitants say that their winter begins at Michaelmas; so that there would probably be much snow on the pass when Hannibal reached it on the 26th of October, as Polybius says. During the summer season, the traffic across the pass is considerable, and mules are constantly passing. The carriage road ends at St. Maurice, on the Savoy side, and at La Tuille on the Italian side. In 1815 an Austrian column of 6,000 men passed by this route, with 10 pieces of cannon. There is no cultivation on the summit, except a little garden which adjoins the hospice. The cold is not so severe as on the Great St. Bernard; and the thermometer, sheltered from the wind and sun, stood at 64° at half-past 9 A. M., on the 24th of August 1819. The road across to Italy leads first through the valley of La Tuille, where it turns E to the valley of Aosta, and from thence proceeds to the Milanese.
BERNARDIERE (LA), a small port and hamlet of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Saint Agnaut, 16 m. WSW of Rochefort.

BERNARDINO (IL), a mountain of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, 12 leagues ESE of St. Gothard, between the valley of Misox or Mœsa and that of the Hinter-Rhine. Its elevation is 1,585 toises or 10,156 ft. The Bernardino forms a line of demarcation between the climate of Germany, and that of Italy. Two different roads, -of which the shortest is practicable only in summer,—run across this range. At the most elevated point of the road is an inn where are warm mineral baths. Near this point is the Muesa, a small lake. The stream which descends from the southern side of the glacier of the Rhine falls into this lake, of which the outlet forms the ravine of Muesa. The B. mountain is composed of gneiss, intermingled with thick veins of quartz. In March 1799, the French, under General Lecourbe, crossed this range to attack the Austrians.
BERNARDO (San), a village of Mexico, overlooking the great defile near Zacualtipan.—Also a village in New California, on the Zaguanas river, 350 m. NW of Santa Fé.
BERNARDO (Isles of San), a group of islets, 11 in number, off the coast of Ecuador. The N isle is in N lat. 9° 48', W long. 75° 51'.
BERNARDSTON, a township in Franklin co., in the state of Massachusetts, U.S., 99 m. W by N of Boston. Pop. 992.
BERNARDSWILLER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut Rhin, cant. of Obernai. Pop. 1,428.

BERNAU, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, on the Nab, E of Neuhaus. Pop. 1,204.—Also a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, in the circle of Lower Barnim, 18 m. N of Berlin. Pop. in 1837, 2,672.
BERNAVILLE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, arrond. of Doullens .- The BER cant. comprises 27 com.—The com., 8 m. WSW of Doullens, has a pop. of 1,060.

BERNAY, an arrondissement, canton, and commune of France, in the dep. of L'Eure.—The arrond. has an area of 109,264 hectares; and comprises the 6 cant. of Beaumesnil, Beaumont-le-Roger, B., Brionne, Broglie, and Thiberville. Pop. in 1836, 83,106; in 1846, 80,017.—The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,043.—The com. and town, 28 m. WNW of Evreux, on the Charentonne, had a pop. of 4,108 in 1789; 7,244 in 1836; and 10,974 in 1846. The town possesses a tribunal of commerce, and a communal college. It has manufactures of leather, ribbons, hosiery, and cotton stuffs; and conducts an active trade in grain, cattle, horses, iron, paper, cyder, and candles.—Also a com. in the dep. of Sarthe, 12 m. WNW of Le Maux, on the Vegre. Pop. 644.-Also a com. in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Rue. Pop. 574.

BERNBURG, a town of Germany, the cap. of the duchy of Anhalt-Bernburg, on the river Saale, which intersects it, 15 m. above its confluence with the Elbe. It consists of the Old and the New town, on the l. bank of the river; and the Bergstadt, on the r. bank. These are connected by a bridge 173 ft. in length. Pop. 6,000.
BERNDTEN, a town of Prussian Saxony, in the circle of Erfurt, SW of Nordhausen.
BERNE. See BERN.

BERNE, an amt, parish, and village of Germany, in the duchy of Oldenburg, 12 m. NNW of Delmenhorst, on the Berne. Pop. of amt or bailiwick, 7,951; of the p., 3,357; of the v., 488.—Also a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Le Faouet. Pop. 2,648.

BERNE, a township in Albany co., in the state of New York, U.S., 23 m. W of Albany, watered by Foxes creek. Pop. 2,740. - Also a township in Athens co., in the state of Ohio, watered by Federal creek. Pop. 390.—Also a township in Berks co., Pennsylvania, on the Schuylkill. Pop. 3,149.

BERNECK, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, 16 m. NNE of Baireuth, on the White Main, and at the base of the Fichtelgebirge. Pop. 1,024. Iron and beautiful serpentine are mined here; and pearls are fished in the Olsnitz, a stream which falls into the Main at this town. There are also alum and vitriol works.-Also a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Black Forest, 8 m. NW of Nagold. Pop. 489.
BERNECK, or Bernang, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Saint-Gall, in the Lower Rheinthal. Pop. 2,119. Embroidered muslins are manufactured here. Some wine is also made.

BERNERA, the southmost of the Barra group of islands in the Hebrides. It is a mass of gneiss, with the NW part dipping into the water, and the SE exhibiting an abrupt section rising to the height of 500 ft. The cliffs are greatly varied in outline, -inclining, perpendicular, and projecting,-smooth, largely fissured, or minutely intersected, -here overhanging the deep in a jutting mass,—there forming a retiring cove terminating above in a perpendicular fissure, and below in a gloomy cavern, the abode of the darkwinged cormorant. In the summer-months these cliffs are inhabited by prodigious numbers of kittiwakes, guillemots, auks, and puffins. One who has not seen some of the great breeding-places of the Hebrides, can hardly form an idea of the prodigious swarms of birds by which they are frequented. -Also an island in the sound of Harris, lying between North Uist and Pabbay; about 1 m. E of the former, and 5 m. SW of the latter.—Also an island on the W coast of the isle of Lewis, formed by Loch Bernera, and Loch Roag, inlets of the sea which in- THE POPULATION OF THE POPULATION OF THE PARTY AND THE PARTY AND THE POPULATION OF THE POPULATION OF THE POPULATION OF THE POPULATION OF THE POPULATION OF THE POPULATION OF THE POPULATION OF THE POPULATION OF THE POPULATION dent the mainland of Lewis in this quarter. It is about 8 m. in length, by 2 m. in breadth; and is surrounded by an archipelago of islets, amongst which is one to the west of Bernera, known as Little Bernera. Near the shore of the larger Bernera are some interesting monuments, of the kind commonly called Druidical.

BERNEUIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, cant. of Barbezieux. Pop. 1,045 .- Also a com. in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Gemozac. Pop. 1,413.—Also a com. in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, cant. of Nantral. Pop. 1,026.
BERNHARD. See BERNARD.
BERNHARD (SANCT), a village of Lower Austria, in the circle of Krems. Pop. 421.
BERNHARDSVALD, a village of Bavaria, in the Upper Pfalz circle, 3 m. from Pettenrent. Pop. 315. Maximilian and Albert of Bavaria here defeated the elector-palatine in 1504.
BERNHAUSEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the Neckar circle, 6 m. SSE of Stuttgardt, on the 1. bank of the Neckar. Pop. 1,491.
BERNIER (CAPE), a high conical promontory on the E coast of Van Diemen's Land, in S lat. 42° 46' 5".

BERNIER'S ISLAND, a small island off the W coast of New Holland, at the entrance of Shark's bay, in S lat. 25° 29', E long. 112° 27'. It is formed of horizontal beds of sand and limestone, containing shells, and laid upon each other with great regularity.
BERNIERES-SUR-MER, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Douvres. Pop. 1,482.
BERNIERES-LE-PATRY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Vassy. Pop. 1,423.

BERNINA, a mountain-ridge of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, belonging to the Rhetian Alps. A pass communicating between the Upper Engadine and the Valteline, by the valley of Peschiavo, crosses this mountain at an alt. of 2,034 metres, or 6,693 ft. On the top of the ridge are some small lakes.
BERNIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Vauvert. Pop. 1,122.
BERNIZET (CAPE or PEAK), a high rounded promontory, on the S coast of the island of Saghalien, on Aniva bay, in N lat. 47° 33'.

BERNKASTEL, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, the cap. of the circle of the same name, 24 m. NE of Treves, on the Moselle. Pop. 2,100 in 1837. It has an active trade in wine. Copper and lead are mined in the environs. It is the Castellum Tabernarum of the Middle ages.
BERNOS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Bazas. Pop. 1,217.
BERNOS, or Maniana Petrin, a branch of the Balkan chain, running off from the Perserin range, and forming in part the boundary betwixt Albania and Macedonia.
BERNOT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Guise. Pop. 1,202. Silk and cotton goods are woven in this com.

BERNOUILLE (CAPE), a point on the coast of New Holland, to the SE of Kangaroo island, in S lat. 36° 57', E long. 139° 41\frac{1}{3}' [Flinders]; S lat. 37° 01/4, E long. 139° 423/ [Freycinet]. It is a piece of sandy land, rising from the beach to a moderatelyelevated and well-wooded ridge.
BERNSDORF, a village of Prussia, in Silesia, in the gov. of Liegnitz, circle of Hoyerswerda. There are iron-forges here.

BERNSTADT, a town of Prussia, in Silesia, in the gov. and 26 m. E of Breslau, on the Weida. Pop. in 1837, 3,381. The prince of Ols has a castle here.—Also a town of Saxony, in the circle of Bu- BER dissin, 12 m. N of Zittau, on the Pliesnitz. Pop. 1,610.—Also a town of Wurtemberg, in the Danube circle, NW of Albeck. Pop. 724. BERNSTEIN,—in Hungarian, Borostyankö,—a town of Hungary, in the circle of Eisenburg, 12 m. W of Köszeg. Pop. 2,000. Flax and lint are extensively cultivated here; and pyrites are wrought.- Also a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, in the gov. and 60 m. NE of Frankfort, on a lake formed by the Faule-Ihna. Pop. in 1837, 1,523.
BERNSTEIN-AM-WALDE, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, NE of Kronach. Pop. 235.
BERORZHEIM, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle Franconia, on the r. bank of the Altmühl. Pop. 912.
BEROU-LA-MULOTTIE'RE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, cant. of Brezolles. Pop. 420. Paper is manufactured here.
BERRACOI, a district of Persia, in Central Kurdistan, in the beylik of Hakkari, containing 17 villages, with an estimated pop. of 2,720, chiefly Chaldean Christians.
BERRAVOL, a town of Afghanistan, in a long narrow valley in the Eusufzye country, near a stream of the same name, in N lat. 35° 32'. The stream is a tributary of the Lundye, on the W bank.

BERRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, arrond. of Aix.- The cant. comprises 6 com.—The com. and town are situated on the NE side of a salt lake of the same name, 18 m. WSW of Aix. Pop. 1,870. The almonds, figs, and oil of this district are celebrated.— The Etang de B., or Etang de Martigues, is about 14 m. in length, by 8 m. in breadth, and communicates with the sea by the canals of Martigues and Tour-de-Bouc. A large quantity of salt is annually manufactured upon its banks; and its fisheries, especially that of eels, are important. It receives the waters of two small rivers.
BERRIAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of Les Vans. Pop. 1,033.
BERRIC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Questembert. Pop. 1,162.
BERRICK-SALOME, a chapelry in the p. of Chalgrove, Oxfordshire, 4 m. NNE of Wallingford. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 234.
BERRIEDALE, a fishing-hamlet in Caithness, 9 m. NE of Helmsdale, at the mouth of an Alpine stream of the same name.
BERRIEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistere, cant. of Huelgout. Pop. 2,211.
BERRIEN, a county in the SW part of the state of Michigan, watered by the St. Joseph, the Papaw, and the Galien. Area 576 sq. m. Pop. 5,011. Capital, St. Joseph.
BERRIEN SPRINGS, a village in Berrien co., on the W bank of the St. Joseph, and 15 m. from its mouth. Pop. 543.
BERRIER-AND-MURRAH, a township in the p. of Greystock, Cumberland, 8 m. W of Penrith. Pop. 127.
BERRIEW, or ABER-RHIW, a parish and township in Montgomeryshire, 5 m. S by W of Welshpool, at the confluence of the Rhiw with the Severn. Pop. of p. 2,259; of t. 188.
BERRIMA, a village in New South Wales, in the county of St. Vincent, on the Wingecarrabee river, a branch of the Wollondilly. It is on the line of the great road from Sydney to Goulbourn.

BERRINGTON, a hamlet in the p. of Chipping- Camden, Gloucestershire. Pop. 158.—Also a p. in Salop, 5 m. SE by S of Shrewsbury. Area 2,920 acres. Pop. 651.—Also a hamlet in the p. and 2 m. W by S of Tenbury, Worcestershire. Pop. 207.-Also a township in the chapelry of Kyloe, co. of Durham. Pop. 316.
BERROW, a parish in Somersetshire, 9½ m. W by S of Axbridge, on Barrow bay. Area 2,310 acres. Pop. 578.—Also a p. in Worcestershire, 6 m. SW of Upton-upon-Severn. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 480.
